STAPHSCOPE Ultimate S. aureus Analysis Report

Gene-Centric Cross-Genome Analysis with Complete Genome Lists

Samples: 107
Tool: STAPHSCOPE Ultimate v2.1.0
University of Ghana Medical School

Executive Summary

📊 Analysis Overview – Gene‑Centric Approach

This report analyzes 107 Staphylococcus aureus genomes using a gene‑centric approach: each resistance or virulence gene is displayed with all genomes that carry it. This makes outbreak tracking and co‑occurrence analysis immediate and transparent.

Why gene‑centric? Traditional sample‑centric tables force you to search sample by sample. Here, you see the full distribution of every gene in seconds.

Key Statistics

Metric Count Details
Total Samples Analyzed107Complete genomic analysis with all databases
MRSA Samples58Methicillin‑resistant S. aureus (carry mecA/mecC)
MSSA Samples49Methicillin‑sensitive S. aureus
Unique MLST Types16Sequence types (population structure)
Unique spa Types25Protein A gene typing – outbreak tracking
Unique SCCmec Types3SCCmec cassette – MRSA lineage marker
AMR Genes260Across AMRfinder, CARD, ResFinder, etc.
Virulence Genes101From VFDB (toxins, adhesins, immune evasion)
BACMET (Biocide/Metal) Genes92Biocide and heavy metal resistance (hospital environment)
Plasmid Replicons34Plasmid types – horizontal gene transfer potential
High‑Risk AMR+Virulence Combos0Samples with both critical AMR and virulence genes

How to Use This Report

Gene‑Centric Tables

In AMR, Virulence, and BACMET tabs, each gene is shown with all genomes that contain it. Use the genome search box to highlight specific isolates across multiple genes.

Section‑Specific Printing

Click the Print button on any section header to generate a clean printout of only that tab – perfect for reports.

Smart Filter Buttons

Use the coloured buttons to instantly filter for mecA, PVL, TSST‑1, enterotoxins and other key genes.

FASTA QC

Assess assembly quality (N50, contig count, GC%) before drawing biological conclusions.

Sample Overview

🧬 Sample Overview – Population Snapshot

This table summarises the three main typing methods for each S. aureus isolate:

  • MLST (Multi‑Locus Sequence Typing) – gold standard for global epidemiology.
  • spa typing – high‑resolution typing based on the spa gene (protein A repeat region).
  • SCCmec type – identifies the staphylococcal cassette chromosome mec, which carries mecA and defines MRSA.

MRSA samples are highlighted in red. Click on column headers to sort.

FASTA Quality Control Metrics

📏 FASTA Quality Control – Why It Matters

Assembly quality directly affects the reliability of typing and gene detection.

  • Number of contigs – lower is better; fragmented assemblies may miss genes or break up SCCmec cassettes.
  • N50 – the contig length such that 50% of the assembly is in contigs ≥ N50; higher N50 indicates better continuity.
  • GC% – S. aureus is typically 32‑33%; large deviations may indicate contamination.
  • Total length – should be ~2.8 Mbp for a complete genome.

MLST Analysis

🔬 MLST (Multi‑Locus Sequence Typing)

MLST indexes internal fragments of seven housekeeping genes (arcC, aroE, glpF, gmk, pta, tpi, yqiL). Each unique combination of alleles defines a Sequence Type (ST).

Why MLST? It is highly reproducible and portable, enabling global surveillance. Closely related STs belong to the same clonal complex (CC). Major epidemic clones include CC1, CC5, CC8, CC22, CC30 and CC45.

16 unique STs identified.

📊 ST Distribution

spa Typing Analysis

🧬 spa Typing – High‑Resolution Outbreak Tracking

The spa gene encodes protein A, an important virulence factor. The repeat region is highly polymorphic; differences in repeat patterns define spa types.

Why spa typing? It is faster and cheaper than MLST, with higher discrimination. It is widely used for outbreak investigation and surveillance of MRSA.

25 unique spa types identified.

SCCmec Typing Analysis

🧬 SCCmec Typing – The MRSA Cassette

The staphylococcal cassette chromosome mec (SCCmec) carries the mecA or mecC gene, conferring methicillin resistance. SCCmec types are classified by their ccr (cassette chromosome recombinase) gene complex and mec gene complex.

Major types: I, II, III (hospital‑acquired MRSA, often large), IV, V (community‑acquired MRSA, smaller, more mobile).

3 unique SCCmec types identified.

📊 SCCmec Type Distribution

SCCmec TypeCountPercentageCommon STsCommon spa Types
SCCmec_type_V(5C2)3763.8%ST772, ST7669, Not typedt345, t657

SCCmec_type_IV(2B)46.9%ST5, ST121, ST772t311, t657, t314

MRSA Analysis

⚠️ MRSA (Methicillin‑Resistant S. aureus) – A Clinical Priority

MRSA is resistant to all β‑lactam antibiotics, including penicillins and cephalosporins. It is a major cause of hospital‑ and community‑acquired infections. The presence of mecA or mecC (carried on SCCmec) defines MRSA.

58 MRSA samples identified.

📊 MRSA vs MSSA Distribution

StatusCountPercentageCommon STsCommon SCCmec Types
MRSA5854.2%Not typed, ST1, ST121, ST152, ST5, ST7669, ST772, ST789, ST8, ST88SCCmec_type_IV(2B), SCCmec_type_V(5C2&5), SCCmec_type_V(5C2)
MSSA4945.8%Not typed, ST1, ST121, ST15, ST152, ST2434, ST30, ST5, ST508, ST669, ST789, ST8, ST94None

Antimicrobial Resistance Genes

🧬 Antimicrobial Resistance (AMR) Genes – Gene‑Centric View

Each AMR gene is shown with all genomes that contain it. Critical S. aureus AMR genes (mecA, mecC, vanA, vanB) are highlighted. Use the filter buttons to focus on specific resistance classes.

The genome list is scrollable scollable, you can see every sample.

Role of each AMR gene family in S. aureus:
• mecA/mecC – Methicillin resistance (MRSA), confers resistance to all β‑lactam antibiotics.
• vanA/vanB – Vancomycin resistance, last‑line antibiotic for MRSA.
• erm – Macrolide, lincosamide, streptogramin B resistance (MLSB).
• msrA – Macrolide efflux pump.
• mphC – Macrolide phosphotransferase.
• tet – Tetracycline resistance (efflux or ribosomal protection).
• aac/aph/ant – Aminoglycoside modifying enzymes (gentamicin, kanamycin, tobramycin).
• dfr – Trimethoprim resistance.
• cat – Chloramphenicol resistance.

All AMR Genes Across Databases

Virulence Genes

🧬 Virulence Factors – Gene‑Centric View

Each virulence gene is shown with all genomes that contain it. Critical S. aureus virulence genes (PVL, TSST‑1, enterotoxins) are highlighted. Use the filter buttons to focus on specific virulence mechanisms.

The genome list is scrollable you can see every sample.

Role of each virulence factor in S. aureus:
• PVL (lukF/S-PV) – Panton‑Valentine leukocidin, causes leukocyte destruction, linked to severe skin/soft tissue infections.
• TSST‑1 (tsst) – Toxic shock syndrome toxin.
• Enterotoxins (sea‑see, seg‑seu) – Superantigens causing food poisoning and toxic shock.
• Exfoliative toxins (eta, etb) – Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome.
• Hemolysins (hla, hlb, hlg, hld) – Lyse red blood cells and contribute to tissue damage.
• Biofilm (icaADBC) – Polysaccharide intercellular adhesin, critical for device‑related infections.
• Immune evasion (scn, eap, ads) – Inhibit complement, neutrophil chemotaxis, and adenosine signalling.
• Adhesins (clfA/B, fnbA/B) – Bind to fibrinogen and fibronectin, promoting attachment to host tissues.
• NB: If your favourite button is missing, please reach out to brownbeckley94@gmail.com. All suggestions are welcome!

Virulence Databases Summary

VFDB

101 unique virulence genes (Total occurrences: 8228)

Top genes: sdrE (107), sspC (107), sspB (107)...

BACMET: Biocide & Heavy Metal Resistance

🧪 BACMET: Biocide & Heavy Metal Resistance – Environmental Co‑Selection

BACMET2 genes provide resistance to hospital disinfectants (e.g., quaternary ammonium compounds – qac genes) and heavy metals (mer for mercury, ars for arsenic, cop for copper). These markers can co‑select for antibiotic resistance and promote persistence in healthcare settings.

Each gene is shown with all genomes that carry it. Use the filter buttons to explore specific resistance categories.

The genome list is scrollable – all samples are visible.

Environmental co‑selection – why these genes matter:
• qac family – Quaternary ammonium compounds (disinfectants used in hospitals).
• cep – Chlorhexidine resistance (antiseptic).
• mer/ars/cop/sil – Resistance to mercury, arsenic, copper, silver – often linked to metal‑based antimicrobials.
• czc/cad/znt – Efflux of zinc, cadmium, cobalt – co‑selection with antibiotic resistance.
• soxR/cpxR/baeR – Stress response regulators that also upregulate multidrug efflux pumps.
• emr/sme/norA – Multidrug efflux pumps that can export both biocides and antibiotics.

📋 BACMET Genes (Biocide / Heavy Metal Resistance)

Gene Database Count Percentage Genomes (scrollable)

Plasmid Replicon Analysis

🧬 Plasmid Replicons – Horizontal Gene Transfer

Plasmid replicons indicate the presence of specific plasmid types. Plasmids are major vehicles for spreading AMR and virulence genes (e.g., mecA on SCCmec is not a plasmid, but many resistance genes like erm, tet, and vanA are plasmid‑borne).

Each replicon is shown with all genomes that carry it (scrollable list).

AI Assistant Guide

🤖 AI Assistant Guide – How to Use with ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini

You can upload this HTML report (or its JSON data) to AI tools to ask detailed questions about your S. aureus dataset.

Example Questions

  • What are the most common MLST sequence types in this dataset?
  • Which spa types are dominant in MRSA vs MSSA?
  • How many samples carry mecA? What are their STs and spa types?
  • Are there any samples with vancomycin resistance genes (vanA/vanB)?
  • Which samples carry the PVL toxin genes (lukF/S-PV)?
  • List all samples with TSST-1 (tsst).
  • Show me the triple typing combinations (ST-spa-SCCmec) with more than 2 isolates.
  • Which resistance genes co-occur most frequently?

How to Upload Data

Save the staphscope_ultimate_report.json file and upload it to the AI. Most AI tools accept file uploads, or you can copy‑paste relevant tables.

Citations & References

📚 How to Cite StaphScope and Its Dependencies

If you use StaphScope in your research, please cite the main tool and the relevant third‑party tools and databases.

Main StaphScope Citation

Beckley, B., Amarh, V. (2026). StaphScope: a species‑optimized computational pipeline for rapid and accessible Staphylococcus aureus genotyping and surveillance. BMC Genomics, 27:123.

DOI: 10.1186/s12864-026-12609-x

    @article{beckley2026staphscope,
    title={StaphScope: a species‑optimized computational pipeline for rapid and accessible Staphylococcus aureus genotyping and surveillance},
    author={Beckley, Brown and Amarh, Vincent},
    journal={BMC Genomics},
    volume={27},
    pages={123},
    year={2026},
    doi={10.1186/s12864-026-12609-x}
    }

MLST (Torsten Seemann)

https://github.com/tseemann/mlst

    @software{seemann_mlst_2018,
    author = {Seemann, T.},
    title = {MLST: Scan contig files against traditional PubMLST typing schemes},
    year = {2018},
    publisher = {GitHub},
    url = {https://github.com/tseemann/mlst}
    }

PubMLST (Jolley et al.)

https://pubmlst.org

    @article{jolley_pubmlst_2018,
    author = {Jolley, K. A. and Bray, J. E. and Maiden, M. C. J.},
    title = {Open-access bacterial population genomics: {BIGSdb} software, the {PubMLST.org} website and their applications},
    journal = {Wellcome Open Research},
    volume = {3},
    pages = {124},
    year = {2018},
    doi = {10.12688/wellcomeopenres.14826.1}
    }

ABRicate (Torsten Seemann)

https://github.com/tseemann/abricate

    @software{seemann_abricate_2018,
    author = {Seemann, T.},
    title = {ABRicate: Mass screening of contigs for antimicrobial resistance and virulence genes},
    year = {2018},
    publisher = {GitHub},
    url = {https://github.com/tseemann/abricate}
    }

AMRFinderPlus (NCBI)

NCBI AMRFinderPlus

    @article{feldgarden_amrfinderplus_2019,
    author = {Feldgarden, M. et al.},
    title = {AMRFinderPlus and the Reference Gene Catalog facilitate examination of the genomic links among antimicrobial resistance, stress response, and virulence},
    journal = {Scientific Reports},
    volume = {11},
    pages = {12728},
    year = {2019},
    doi = {10.1038/s41598-021-91456-0}
    }

spa Typing (Ridom)

https://spa.ridom.de

Database statistics (May 2026): 22,638 spa types, 863 repeats, 472,120 total strains, from 181 countries.

    @article{mellmann_spa_typing_2005,
    author = {Mellmann, A. et al.},
    title = {Evidenzbasierte Hygienemassnahmen mittels spa-Typisierung bei MRSA-Häufungen im Krankenhaus},
    journal = {Deutsche Medizinische Wochenschrift},
    volume = {130},
    number = {22},
    pages = {1364-1368},
    year = {2005},
    doi = {10.1055/s-2005-868351}
    }

SCCmecFinder (CGE)

CGE SCCmecFinder

    @article{kaya_sccmecfinder_2018,
    author = {Kaya, H. et al.},
    title = {SCCmecFinder, a Web-Based Tool for Typing of Staphylococcal Cassette Chromosome mec in Staphylococcus aureus Using Whole-Genome Sequence Data},
    journal = {mSphere},
    volume = {3},
    number = {1},
    pages = {e00612-17},
    year = {2018},
    doi = {10.1128/mSphere.00612-17}
    }

spa Typing (Ridom)

https://spa.ridom.de

    @article{mellmann_spa_typing_2005,
    author = {Mellmann, A. et al.},
    title = {Evidenzbasierte Hygienemassnahmen mittels spa-Typisierung bei MRSA-Häufungen im Krankenhaus},
    journal = {Deutsche Medizinische Wochenschrift},
    volume = {130},
    number = {22},
    pages = {1364-1368},
    year = {2005},
    doi = {10.1055/s-2005-868351}
    }

CARD

Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database

    @article{alcock_card_2023,
    author = {Alcock, B. P. et al.},
    title = {CARD 2023: expanded curation, support for machine learning, and resistome prediction at the Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database},
    journal = {Nucleic Acids Research},
    volume = {51},
    number = {D1},
    pages = {D690-D699},
    year = {2023},
    doi = {10.1093/nar/gkac920}
    }

ResFinder

    @article{bortolaia_resfinder_2020,
    author = {Bortolaia, V. et al.},
    title = {ResFinder 4.0 for predictions of phenotypes from genotypes},
    journal = {Journal of Antimicrobial Chemotherapy},
    volume = {75},
    number = {12},
    pages = {3491-3500},
    year = {2020},
    doi = {10.1093/jac/dkaa345}
    }

VFDB – Virulence Factor Database

    @article{chen_vfdb_2016,
    author = {Chen, L. et al.},
    title = {VFDB 2016: hierarchical and refined dataset for big data analysis—10 years on},
    journal = {Nucleic Acids Research},
    volume = {44},
    number = {D1},
    pages = {D694-D697},
    year = {2016},
    doi = {10.1093/nar/gkv1239}
    }

PlasmidFinder

    @article{carattoli_plasmidfinder_2014,
    author = {Carattoli, A. et al.},
    title = {In silico detection and typing of plasmids using PlasmidFinder and plasmid multilocus sequence typing},
    journal = {Antimicrobial Agents and Chemotherapy},
    volume = {58},
    number = {7},
    pages = {3895-3903},
    year = {2014},
    doi = {10.1128/AAC.02412-14}
    }

MEGARes 3.0

    @article{bonin_megares_2023,
    author = {Bonin, N. et al.},
    title = {MEGARes and AMR++, v3.0: an updated comprehensive database of antimicrobial resistance determinants and an improved software pipeline for classification using high-throughput sequencing},
    journal = {Nucleic Acids Research},
    volume = {51},
    number = {D1},
    pages = {D744-D752},
    year = {2023},
    doi = {10.1093/nar/gkac1047}
    }
Suggested acknowledgement:
“Genomic analysis was performed using StaphScope [Beckley & Amarh, 2026], which integrates MLST [Seemann, 2018] using the Pubmlst database [Jolley et al., 2018], ABRicate [Seemann, 2018], AMRFinderPlus [Feldgarden et al., 2019], and SCCmecFinder [Kaya et al., 2018] for comprehensive S. aureus characterization. Antimicrobial resistance genes were identified using the CARD [Alcock et al., 2023] and ResFinder [Bortolaia et al., 2020] databases.”

Call to Action

🚀 Call to Action – Help Us Fight AMR!

If you find this report useful, please star our GitHub repository and share it with your colleagues. Your support drives further development (and keeps us caffeinated).

⭐ Star us on GitHub

https://github.com/bbeckley-hub/staphscope-typing-tool

The ESKAPE Rogues' Gallery – Our Other Tools

We’ve built a whole zoo of pathogen‑specific pipelines – each one more obsessive about your favourite bug. Behold, the ESKAPE lineup (because one bug report is never enough):

  • EnteroMark – for Enterococcus faecium (VRE surveillance, the "E" in ESKAPE): github.com/bbeckley-hub/EnteroMark
  • 🧫 StaphScope – for Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA, the "S" – you're using it right now!): github.com/bbeckley-hub/staphscope-typing-tool
  • 🧬 Kleboscope – for Klebsiella pneumoniae (carbapenemase hunter, the "K"): github.com/bbeckley-hub/Kleboscope
  • 🧪 Acinetoscope – for Acinetobacter baumannii (WHO Critical Priority, the "A"): github.com/bbeckley-hub/acinetoscope
  • 🧴 Pseudoscope – for the mighty Pseudomonas aeruginosa (biofilm king, the "P"): github.com/bbeckley-hub/pseudoscope
  • 🧫 Enteroscope – for Enterobacter cloacae (the forgotten ESKAPE member, the final "E"): github.com/bbeckley-hub/enteroscope

And because we like to go the extra mile (and bug you), we also have EcoliTyper for E. coli serotyping – because even non‑ESKAPE bugs deserve love: github.com/bbeckley-hub/EcoliTyper

If you like what we’re doing, give them a ⭐ too – it helps us stay motivated (and caffeinated). We put the "fun" in "fungus" (even though most of these are bacteria).

Collaborate & Contribute – Join the Resistance!

We welcome bug reports, feature requests, and collaborations. Open an issue on GitHub or email brownbeckley94@gmail.com.

University of Ghana Medical School – Putting African genomics on the map, one genome at a time.

Fun fact: The ESKAPE pathogens are responsible for the majority of hospital‑acquired infections and are notoriously multi‑drug resistant. Our tools help you fight back – with code, not just antibiotics.

Together we can beat AMR – one genome at a time. Now go forth and type!

Export Data

📥 Export Data – Download Your Results

Download all analysis tables in CSV format or the complete JSON data for downstream use.

Sample Overview CSV

All samples with MLST, spa, SCCmec, MRSA status

AMR Genes CSV

Gene‑centric AMR table with genome lists

Virulence Genes CSV

Gene‑centric virulence table

BACMET Genes CSV

Biocide & heavy metal resistance genes

Plasmid Replicons CSV

Plasmid replicon distribution

FASTA QC CSV

Assembly quality metrics

Complete JSON Data

All analysis data in structured JSON format

Note: The JSON file (staphscope_ultimate_report.json) is saved in the same directory as this HTML report. You can use it for custom scripts or upload to AI tools.

STAPHSCOPE Ultimate S. aureus Reporter v2.1.0

University of Ghana Medical School | Brown Beckley

Please give a big STAR on GitHub if you found the integrated report useful.
